coredata.dc:description: This study introduces an environmentally sustainable method for synthesizing a nanocomposite using carbon quantum dots derived from green tea waste and zinc oxide. The single-step hydrothermal process not only addresses waste management but also yields a versatile nanocomposite with diverse applications. Rigorous characterization reveals its morphology, crystallinity, phase identification, structural behavior, optical properties, and chemical composition. Incorporating carbon quantum dots into the zinc oxide matrix reduces the band gap to 1.87 eV, enhancing charge separation and light-harvesting. This modification significantly boosts photocatalytic activity, achieving degradation efficiencies of 95.16 % for methylene blue (MB) and 93.21 % for congo red (CR) under visible light. The effects of pH, contact time, and photocatalyst concentration on dye degradation were explored. The nanocomposite, exhibiting both adsorption and photocatalytic performance, is poised for broad applications. Fluorescence quenching studies with lysozyme protein provide insights into potential applications across diverse fields, highlighting its environmentally friendly and multifunctional attributes.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study introduces an environmentally sustainable method for synthesizing a nanocomposite using carbon quantum dots derived from green tea waste and zinc oxide. The single-step hydrothermal process not only addresses waste management but also yields a versatile nanocomposite with diverse applications. Rigorous characterization reveals its morphology, crystallinity, phase identification, structural behavior, optical properties, and chemical composition. Incorporating carbon quantum dots into the zinc oxide matrix reduces the band gap to 1.87 eV, enhancing charge separation and light-harvesting. This modification significantly boosts photocatalytic activity, achieving degradation efficiencies of 95.16 % for methylene blue (MB) and 93.21 % for congo red (CR) under visible light. The effects of pH, contact time, and photocatalyst concentration on dye degradation were explored. The nanocomposite, exhibiting both adsorption and photocatalytic performance, is poised for broad applications. Fluorescence quenching studies with lysozyme protein provide insights into potential applications across diverse fields, highlighting its environmentally friendly and multifunctional attributes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the contemporary era, energy scarcity and environmental pollution are two paramount global challenges, serving as foundational barriers to the sustainable advancement of human society [1]. Consequently, the exploration of renewable energy and the development of efficient technologies to tackle energy limitations and environmental degradation have emerged as crucial tasks requiring research-driven solutions [2]. Photocatalysis is regarded as a valuable technique for addressing environmental and energy concerns, given its ability to either harness solar energy to the maximum extent or transform solar energy into chemical energy [3,4]. Utilizing metal-oxide semiconductor materials, photocatalysis technology stands out as one of the most promising alternatives. For the efficient removal of organic, inorganic, and microbial contaminants, heterogeneous semiconductor-based photocatalysis is essential. Furthermore, the use of heterogeneous photocatalysts reduces the development of secondary impurities [5–7].

SnO2, TiO2, WO3, Fe3O4, and ZnO are among the numerous metal oxide semiconductors employed in photocatalytic degradation. These materials possess band gap energies spanning 2-4 eV and can generate electron-hole pairs (EHP) using both ultraviolet (UV) and visible light wavelengths [8,9]. In contrast to other metal oxides, ZnO offers several benefits, including thermal stability, simple manufacturability, non-toxicity, extensive availability, cost-effectiveness, high electron mobility and photostability [10]. Due to its predominant activity in the UV range, ZnO absorbs only a small fraction (3–5 %) of solar light energy. This characteristic limits its band gap into the visible spectrum, ultimately enhancing its photocatalytic activity [11]. Nonetheless, the practical implementation of ZnO faces challenges such as limited utilization of solar light caused by a wide bandgap, reduced quantum efficiency, and rapid recombination of EHP [12]. Diverse approaches have been formulated to enhance carrier separation efficiency, with the creation of heterostructure photocatalysts emerging as a promising avenue [13,14]. The establishment of this heterojunction aids in suppressing the recombination of EHP, leading to an augmentation in photocatalytic efficiency [15]. The ZnO nanostructure can be improved through the use of metal or nonmetal dopants, noble metal coatings, heterostructure constructions, and the combination of carbon-based materials [16–18]. Out of these possibilities, the combination of carbon based materials has been acknowledged as a beneficial tactic for facilitating the separation of photo-induced EHP and enhancing the semiconductor materials' redox capabilities [19,20]. The significant trend of integrating carbon materials with ZnO has arisen, primarily attributed to their function as photoelectron reservoirs responsible for capturing and transporting photogenerated electrons from ZnO to substrates. Furthermore, their photosensitizer characteristics extend ZnO's light absorption range into visible and near-infrared spectra [10].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a recently unveiled quasi-zero-dimensional nanomaterial, have captured the attention of scientists owing to their amenable exterior surface, high optical absorption coefficient, and chemically inert biocompatible nature. The distinctive up-conversion luminescence exhibited by CQDs can be triggered by higher wavelengths and lower frequencies of light, subsequently emitting shorter-wavelength light at higher frequencies. Furthermore, the addition of nonmetallic elements to CQDs can result in outstanding catalytic capabilities increased quantum yields, and improved optical properties [21,22]. Additionally, CDs can serve as electron acceptors when combined with a semiconductor photocatalyst, effectively reducing the rate at which photogenerated EHP recombine. This dual action results in increased optical absorption and consequently boosts photocatalytic activity [10]. Recently, numerous studies have focused on the application of CQDs-decorated ZnO materials in the field of photocatalysis [23–25]. For instance, Qu et al. pioneered the development of a composite material consisting of ZnO and N,S-CQDs. This innovative composite demonstrated its efficacy in the degradation of MB under the influence of visible light [25]. Similarly, Li et al. developed a ZnO and CQDs nanocomposite that exhibited remarkable efficiency in degrading rhodamine B RhB when exposed to UV light [26]. These composite structures showed improved photocatalytic performance in the relevant experimental contexts.

In this context, waste green tea bags were employed as the source of carbon to synthesize biomass-derived CQDs through a hydrothermal process. Subsequently, the CQDs were incorporated into ZnO, culminating in the creation of a composite material designated as CQZ. The combination of CQDs and ZnO is suitable for enhancing charge separation by limiting charge recombination and promoting photocatalytic efficiency. The resulting heterostructure nanocomposite exhibits deliberately modified surface texture, improved optical and electronic characteristics, and adjustable band gap. Under visible light conditions, the nanocomposite material shows enhanced photocatalytic efficacy. Moreover, it displays remarkable stability in degrading MB and CR, indicating its potential for effectively eliminating synthetic pigment-based pollutants. A plausible mechanism for the photocatalytic degradation process has been delineated to elucidate how EHP generated by light are transferred on the surface of the nanocomposite. Additionally, fluorescence quenching was utilized as a methodology to explore the interactions between the nanocomposite and lysozyme protein. This investigative approach involved systematically measuring and analyzing changes in the fluorescence emission of the nanocomposite, serving as a sensitive probe to discern alterations in the microenvironment surrounding the fluorophores upon interaction with the lysozyme protein. The observed quenching phenomena provide valuable insights into the binding affinity, conformational alterations, and dynamic behavior of the nanocomposite in the presence of lysozyme, thereby elucidating the nature of their molecular interactions. To the best of our knowledge, no existing literature or reports have addressed the utilization of this specific nanocomposite for the degradation of both CR and MB dyes, nor have investigations explored into its interactions with lysozyme protein.

Zinc nitrate hexahydrate [(Zn(NO₃)₂·6H₂O] and sodium hydroxide pellets (NaOH) were procured from Sigma-Aldrich. Green tea bags were sourced as used materials. Congo red and methylene blue were acquired from CDH India.

ZnO nanoparticles (NPs) were synthesized via a hydrothermal method. A 0.5 M solution of Zn(NO3)2 was meticulously prepared in 30 mL of distilled water with continuous stirring lasting for 40 min. Concurrently, a 5 M NaOH solution was formulated by dissolving NaOH pellets in an equivalent volume of distilled water, subjected to stirring for the same duration. With persistent agitation, incremental drops of the NaOH solution were judiciously added to the Zn(NO3)2 solution until the pH level reached 12. Subsequently, the resulting solution was introduced into a hydrothermal Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated to 120 °C for 2 h. Following the hydrothermal treatment, the mixture was allowed to cool down to room temperature. The resultant product was filtered, washed with distilled water, and subsequently dried in an oven [27,28].

CQDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal method utilizing waste green tea bags as the carbon precursor. The procedure began with the preliminary treatment of the green tea bags, which involved drying them and then grinding using a mortar and pestle. Subsequently, a solution was prepared by subjecting 10 g of green tea powder to ultrasonic treatment in 50 mL of distilled water for 30 min. The resulting solution was then introduced into a hydrothermal reactor and heated to 220 °C for 3 h within in an oven. Following the hydrothermal treatment, the reaction mixture was allowed to cool to ambient temperature (27 °C), and larger particles were separated from the resulting solution through filtration using a micro-pore filter with a pore size of 0.22 μ. Finally, the resulting dispersion was stored at 4 °C for subsequent analysis and application.

The formation of the nanocomposite (CQZ) involved dispersing CQDs (2 mL) and 50 mg of ZnO NPs into 25 mL of ethanol. Subsequently, the mixture was stirred for duration of 45 min at room temperature (Fig. 1 ). The resulting CQZ nanocomposite was then subjected to filtration, followed by multiple rounds of washing with ethanol. Finally, the composite was dried in an oven prior to its utilization [29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of simple mixture of CQDs and ZnO NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Disperse 2 mL of pre-synthesized CQDs into 25 mL of ethanol. Ensure uniform distribution of the CQDs by stirring the solution for 10 min at room temperature. In a separate preparation, weigh 50 mg of ZnO NPs and disperse them in 25 mL of ethanol. Stir the ZnO suspension for 10 min at room temperature to achieve a homogeneous distribution. After ensuring uniform dispersion of both components, combine the CQD and ZnO suspensions by carefully pouring them together into a single container. Stir the combined suspension gently for 5 min to promote even particle distribution throughout the mixture. Following the mixing process, remove the ethanol solvent by placing the mixture in an oven set to a low temperature (approximately 60–80 °C). Allow the solvent to evaporate completely, resulting in a dry physical mixture of CQDs and ZnO NPs. Store the resulting powder as the physical mixture of CQDs and ZnO NPs.

The quantum yield of CQDs was determined using quinine sulfate (QS) as a standard reference. Quinine sulfate has a known quantum yield (QY) of 0.54 in 0.1 M H2SO4 at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. To ensure accuracy, the absorbance of both the CQDs and quinine sulfate solutions was maintained below 0.1. Quantum yield measurements were calculated using Eq. (1).

where, Q stands for the quantum yield, I and A for the sample's emission intensity and absorbance, respectively, and η for the solvent's refractive index (1.33). The standard reference is denoted by the subscript R. The QY of the synthesized CQDs was found to be 21 %. Additionally, the quantum yield measurement of the prepared nanocomposite showed it to be 23 %.

Photocatalytic experiments were conducted on the synthesized nanocomposite to evaluate its ability to degrade dyes (MB & CR) under visible light [30]. Dye solutions of varying concentrations (5 to 100 mg/L), were prepared from a 1000 ppm stock solution. A calibration curve was generated using a UV–visible spectrophotometer with λmax values of 664 nm for MB, and 498 nm for CR dye, respectively. The photocatalytic reaction proceeded by suspending 1 g of nanocomposite in 25 mL of dye solutions (5–100 mg/L), followed by gentle swirling in the dark for 30 min to ensure adsorption–desorption in equilibrium. Subsequently, 5 mL of the solution was withdrawn, and the catalyst was separated from the mixture via centrifugation before initiating the photodegradation. The assessment of photocatalytic activity was carried out in batch mode, encompassing variations in parameters such as different light sources, dye concentration (5 to 100 mg/L), pH of the medium (2−12), temperature, contact time, and degradation by distinct catalysts [31].

where C0 and A0 are the initial concentration and absorbance of dye while Ct and At are the concentration of dye, and absorbance after time t respectively [3].

The degradation of dyes using a CQZ nanocomposite required identification of reactive species through trapping experiments. During irradiation experiments, various scavengers, namely benzoquinone, isopropyl alcohol, and Na2-EDTA, were used to capture specific reactive species—superoxide radical anion, hydroxyl radical, and hole, respectively, in aqueous dye solutions. Experimental conditions mirrored those of photocatalytic activity tests, with the main difference being the introduction of scavengers before adding the catalyst. The concentration of all scavengers was maintained at a constant 1 mM. Reaction progress was monitored via spectrophotometry, measuring changes in dye solution absorbance. Additionally, hydroxyl radicals generated within the reaction medium were quantitatively evaluated utilizing the photoluminescence probe method with terephthalic acid. In this method, a specific amount of CQZ nanocomposite was dispersed in an aqueous solution containing NaOH (1 mM) and terephthalic acid (0.5 mM) through sonication. Upon excitation at 330 nm, hydroxyl radicals reacted with terephthalic acid, producing a fluorescent adduct as 2-hydroxy terephthalic acid. This fluorescent adduct was traced and quantified spectrofluorimetrically [32].

The investigation of molecular interactions between the nanocomposite and human serum lysozyme involved using a Spectro fluorophotometer (RF-5301PC; Shimadzu, Japan) equipped with a 150 W Xenon lamp. Excitation and emission measurements were conducted with slit widths set at 5 nm, while maintaining a constant temperature of 298 K. The lysozyme protein (HSL) solution was prepared in a phosphate buffer at pH 7.4. To assess the interaction between the nanocomposite and lysozyme protein, variations in the initial fluorescence of HSL were monitored upon the addition of CQDs at concentrations ranging from 1 to 10 μM. Excitation was performed at a wavelength of 280 nm, and the resulting spectra were recorded in the range of 300 to 400 nm [33].

The morphological characteristics and elemental composition of the nanocomposite were systematically examined using advanced analytical techniques. Morphological validation was performed using a scanning electron microscope (SEM), specifically the JSM-6510LV model from JEOL, Japan. Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) images were acquired with a JEOL TEM2100 instrument operating at 200 kV to further investigates the nanocomposite at the nanoscale. The structural properties of the nanocomposite were probed using X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR). XRD elucidated the crystalline structure, while FT-IR identified functional groups and overall composition. The chemical structure and composition were scrutinized using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), conducted on a Theroscientific instrument with a Kα monochromatic source, providing detailed insights into the surface composition and electronic states of the constituent elements. For the assessment of degradation activity and fluorescence properties, a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer) and a spectro fluorophotometer (RF-5301PC; Shimadzu, Japan) were employed. These instruments measured the nanocomposite's response to external stimuli and its fluorescence characteristics. Thermal analysis was conducted using a Shimadzu thermogravimetric analyzer DTG-60H in a platinum crucible under a pure air atmosphere with a constant flow rate of 20 mL/min. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and differential thermal analysis (DTA) curves were obtained over a temperature range from 0 to 700 °C, with a heating rate of 10 °C/min. These analyses provided valuable information regarding the thermal stability and degradation behavior of the nanocomposite.

Adsorption experiments were conducted by agitating an aqueous solution containing MB and CR dyes at concentrations ranging from 5 to 100 ppm in a closed vessel. The agitation process occurred in the presence of a catalyst (1 g) at ambient temperature for duration of 24 h under dark conditions. Each experiment involved diluting the stock solution of MB and CR to varied concentrations using double distilled water. The experimental setup consistently used a 50 mL of dye solution [34]. The adsorption capacity at equilibrium (Qe) and at time t (Qt) is expressed by the following equations:

Here, m represents the adsorbent dosage (g), Ci and Ct (mg/L) denote the initial and final concentrations of dyes, and V is the volume (L) of solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of surface functional groups and crystalline nature
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2(A) depicts the XRD patterns of both ZnO and CQZ nanocomposite. The XRD analysis reveals distinct diffraction peaks attributed to the ZnO NPs. Specifically, peaks are observed at 31.80°, 34°, 36.30°, 47.7°, 56.8°, 63.1° and 66.4°, corresponding to the crystallographic planes (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103) and (200) of the ZnO nanostructure. These observations confirm the presence of wurtzite crystal planes in the ZnO (JCPDS) reference No. 36–1451 [35]. Additionally, the XRD analysis reveals characteristic peaks of CQDs (inset) within the 2θ range of 20–30°, indicative of the amorphous nature of the carbon [36]. Notably, the nanocomposite exhibits sharp diffraction peaks corresponding to the crystalline structure of ZnO, emphasizing the coexistence of crystalline ZnO and amorphous CQDs in the synthesized nanocomposite.

The comparative XRD spectra of the simple physical mixture of CQDs and ZnO and the CQZ nanocomposite are presented in Fig. S-12. In the CQZ nanocomposite, the incorporation of CQDs may induce subtle modifications in the ZnO lattice, which can be detected by shifts, broadening, or changes in the intensity of diffraction peaks in the XRD pattern. In contrast, a physical mixture of CQDs and ZnO would exhibit distinct and separate diffraction patterns characteristic of the individual components, without any indication of lattice interaction or structural integration.

In Fig. 2(B), the FTIR spectra of the ZnO and CQZ nanocomposite reveal distinct vibrational modes that provide crucial information about their composition and structural attributes. The ZnO spectrum displays specific peaks indicative of its properties. The small peak observed at 510 cm−1 corresponds to the ZnO stretching vibrations, confirming its crystalline nature. Additionally, the presence of a peak at 450 cm−1 signifies lattice vibrations, suggesting the characteristic wurtzite structure. The appearance of a peak at 585 cm−1 signifies the bending vibrations of hydroxyl groups present on the surface. In the CQZ nanocomposite spectrum, alongside the ZnO peaks, new peaks emerge at 1600 and 1390 cm−1, characteristic of CC and CO stretching vibrations typically associated with CQDs. Furthermore, peaks at 2952 cm−1 and 1150 cm−1 correspond to stretching vibrations of CH and CO groups, respectively, indicating successful interactions between ZnO and CQDs. These interactions provide valuable insights into the composite's structure and potential applications. The presence of a peak at 3455 cm−1, attributed to the hydroxyl (OH) group, is observed in both the ZnO and CQZ nanocomposite spectra, suggesting the presence of water molecules on the surface of both materials [29,37].The comparative FTIR spectra of the simple physical mixture of CQDs and ZnO and the CQZ nanocomposite are presented in Fig. S-13. The shifts in functional group vibrations observed in the CQZ spectrum, which are absent in the physical mixture, provide strong evidence of chemical bonding or interactions between CQDs and ZnO in the nanocomposite.

Fig. 3(A) illustrates the HRTEM images of the CQZ nanocomposite. The HRTEM images confirm the successful deposition of CQDs onto the surface of ZnO NPs. Due to their inherently hydrophilic properties, the CQDs exhibit a uniform distribution on the ZnO surface, resulting in the formation of a cluster-like structure. To distinguish between the CQDs and ZnO NPs, interplanar spacings were measured. The inset in Fig. 3A reveals interplanar distances of 0.51 nm and 0.32 nm, corresponding to the lattice spacing of wurtzite ZnO and CQDs, respectively [38]. This observation further supports the accurate localization of CQDs on the ZnO surface. The average size of individual particles within the synthesized nanocomposite is approximately 32.5 nm. For a comprehensive understanding of the size distribution, refer to the size distribution curve depicted in Fig. S1B. This curve provides additional insights into the homogeneity and dispersion of particle sizes within the CQZ nanocomposite [11,39,40].

Fig. 3(B) presents SEM images showcasing the morphology of a nanocomposite. The images depict spherical CQDs exhibiting a uniform distribution, resembling a floral arrangement on the surface of ZnO. However, discerning the presence of CQDs solely through SEM imagery poses challenges regarding their distinguishability on the ZnO surface. Consequently, EDX was employed to authenticate the existence of CQDs on the ZnO NPs surface, as depicted in Fig. 3C & D. The appearance of a distinct carbon peak, alongside zinc and oxygen signals, serves as confirmation of CQDs onto the ZnO NPs surface. The EDX analysis substantiates the successful amalgamation of CQDs onto the ZnO NP surface. Furthermore, the electric potential on the CQZ nanocomposite surface was evaluated via zeta potential analysis. The synthesized nanocomposite manifests a negative surface charge, evidenced by a zeta potential value of −9.75 mV (Fig. S2). This negative charge is significant as it can influence the interaction of the nanocomposite with dye molecules present in the surrounding medium [41].

In the CQZ nanocomposite, the CQDs are uniformly anchored onto the surface of ZnO NPs. This uniform distribution arises from the chemical interactions, such as covalent bonding or electrostatic forces, that occur during the synthesis process. These interactions lead to a synergistic effect, enhancing properties like electron transfer, photocatalytic activity, and charge separation. Consequently, CQZ behaves more like a single-phase material, where the CQDs and ZnO NPs are integrated at the molecular level. In contrast, a simple mixture of CQDs and ZnO is likely to exhibit a more heterogeneous structure in SEM images, with the CQDs and ZnO NPs appearing as distinct, unconnected entities. This lack of strong interaction or chemical bonding results in a less cohesive material (Fig. S-14).

TGA investigations were conducted under controlled ambient furnace conditions, where the sample mass was plotted against temperature to elucidate the thermally induced stability profile of the fabricated material. The synthesized nanocomposite underwent controlled heating in an oxygen-rich environment, utilizing a heating ramp of 10 °C/min until reaching a temperature of 800 °C. Fig. 4A & B depicts the TGA-DTA analysis of the CQZ nanocomposite. The first decrease in weight between 50 and 250 °C (∼12.5 %) is attributed to desorption of water molecules adsorbed onto the composite's surface. Furthermore, a weight reduction of approximately 44.12 % occurring between 250 and 1350 °C corresponds to the gradual loss of oxygen-bearing moieties in CQDs within the composite. The weight loss 22.64 % at temperature range 1350-1750 °C is due to the degradation of organic or polymeric component in the nanocomposite (they may start to decompose or burning off in this temperature range, leading to weight loss). In the temperature range from 1750 to 2500 °C weight loss (10.55 %) might be attributed to surface processes or interactions on the ZnO component, such as desorption or breakdown of adsorbed molecules. It is noteworthy that this specific phase of weight loss is absent in pure ZnO samples [42]. In DTA plot relatively small peaks indicate the presence of amorphous and disordered carbon in trace concentrations in the nanocomposite [43]. A prominent exothermic peak at 1600 °C (between 1350 °C to 1755 °C) was observed without any corresponding change in the TGA curve, attributed to the burning of CQDs [44,45].

The photocatalytic efficacy of a material depends on its ability to effectively absorb light and efficiently separate photogenerated charge carriers. Light absorption performance can be assessed using UV–vis spectroscopy, wherein absorption peaks were observed at 275 nm and 330 nm. The peak at 275 nm corresponds to the n-π* transition (C=O), while the peak at 330 nm is attributed to direct band-to-band transitions, involving electrons moving from the valence band to the conduction band in the ZnO NPs (Fig. 5A) [9,46]. As depicted in Fig. 5A (inset), the light absorption spectrum of ZnO NPs is confined to wavelengths below 400 nm, primarily indicating its responsive range within the UV spectrum [47,48]. Conversely, the introduction of CQDs into ZnO composites results in the progressive extension of absorption edges into the visible region. This underscores the propensity of CQDs to enhance light absorption across a broader range of wavelengths, as demonstrated in the UV-DRS spectrum of the CQZ nanocomposite (Fig. 5B). Consequently, the resultant nanocomposites exhibit an augmented light absorption capacity, attributable to the synergistic effects of the combined materials. The band gap, a crucial parameter, is computed using UV data by extrapolating the tangent line through the inflection point in the absorption band and the photon energy. The band gap values for ZnO and CQZ composite are 3.32 eV and 1.8 eV, respectively, as shown in Fig. 5C. The reduced band gap in the nanocomposite signifies an enhanced potential for the comprehensive utilization of solar energy, attributed to the presence of CQDs within the ZnO matrix. Photoluminescence studies (PL) were conducted at room temperature to systematically investigate the optical characteristics of ZnO, CQDs, and CQZ nanocomposite (Fig. 5D). The examination of the photoluminescence spectrum revealed a distinct peak at 370 nm, indicative of a pronounced emission in the ultraviolet (UV) region. This emission phenomenon is ascribed to the vigorous recombination of free excitons occurring in proximity to the band-edge of ZnO. Subsequent analysis focused on the PL intensity of ZnO and CQDs in comparison to the CQZ nanocomposite. Notably, the CQZ nanocomposite exhibited lower photoluminescence intensities compared to both pure ZnO and CQDs. This discrepancy suggests that the incorporation of CQDs into the nanocomposite resulted in an amelioration of the optical properties inherent to ZnO NPs. The observed enhancement in optical properties is rationalized by the introduction of CQDs, which create electron traps within the CQZ nanocomposite. These traps effectively inhibit or suppress recombination centers, thereby retarding the recombination process. Consequently, the photoluminescence properties of the synthesized product are augmented, attesting to the beneficial impact of CQDs on the overall optical behavior of ZnO NPs in the composite material. Conversely, in a simple physical mixture of CQDs and ZnO, the PL spectra of the individual components tend to be additive, with minimal quenching or shifts observed. This lack of spectral alteration suggests that there is no synergistic interaction between the CQDs and ZnO in the mixture, and the two components retain their independent optical characteristics (Fig. S-15).

To comprehensively investigate interfacial bonding configurations and electron redistribution at the interface, XPS analyses were performed. The XPS spectrum of CQDs and ZnO shows the presence of various elemental peaks (Fig. S3). The XPS spectrum of CQDs shows a peak at 285 eV for C1s and at 530 eV for O1s. The high resolution spectrum of C1s exhibits distinct peaks at various binding energies corresponding to different carbon-containing functional groups. A peak at 285.2 eV is attributed to C-O-C groups, indicating the presence of ether or epoxide linkages. Another peak at 287.5 eV corresponds to CO groups, suggesting the presence of carbonyl functionalities. Additionally, a peak at 290.32 eV is associated with CC bonds, which are indicative of conjugated or aromatic carbon structures. The O1s spectrum for CQDs shows a peak at 531.6 eV, which is related to CO groups, and another peak at 534.5 eV, corresponding to C-OH groups, reflecting hydroxyl functionalities on the surface [34]. In the case of ZnO, the high resolution C1s spectrum shows a peak at 284.2 eV, attributed to CC bonds, typical of aliphatic or aromatic carbon. A peak at 288 eV is assigned to CO groups, indicating the presence of carbonyl functionalities. Additionally, a peak at 286.1 eV corresponds to CO groups, suggesting the presence of alcohols or ethers. The Zn spectrum displays characteristic peaks at 1025 eV and 1045 eV, corresponding to the binding energies of zinc. The O1s spectrum for ZnO reveals a peak at 531 eV, associated with ZnO bonds, confirming the presence of zinc oxide. Another peak at 533.5 eV corresponds to chemisorbed oxygen species on the surface of the ZnO, indicating the presence of adsorbed oxygen or hydroxyl groups [35,37].

As illustrated in Fig. 6A, the nanocomposite exhibits three distinct peaks corresponding to carbon, oxygen, and zinc at 286, 532, and 1017 eV, respectively. Elemental composition determination through XPS revealed that the nanocomposite predominantly consisted of carbon (49 %), oxygen (24 %), and zinc (30 %). Further scrutiny of the high-resolution C1s spectrum after mixing (Fig. 6B) manifested peaks at 286.4, 289.1, and 291.8 eV, attributed to various carbon-related bonds. These encompass CC bonds in adventitious carbon, sp2-hybridized carbon inherent to aromatic networks, and oxygen-associated species, including CO and CO groups [49,50] These spectral features collectively suggested the presence of CQDs within the composite configuration. The peak at 284.2 eV can be assigned to ZnC bonds in association with oxygen vacancies or defects. This phenomenon can be attributed to the increased electron density resulting from negatively charged O on the C within the Zn-C-O bond [49–51]. The high resolution spectrum of O1 s (Fig. 6C) displays two peaks at 532 and 529.1 eV, which can be assigned to the surface adsorbed oxygen in OH groups and ZnO bonds [51]. As depicted in Fig. 6D the two peaks located at 1020.96 and 1045.04 eV pertain to the Zn2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 states, resulting from spin-orbital splitting. This observation signifies the presence of Zn2+ ions within the sample, indicative of the presence of ZnO NPs in the nanocomposite configuration.

Based on the findings obtained through curve fitting analysis of C1s, O1s, and Zn 2p spectra, a comprehensive understanding of the interaction mechanism between ZnO and CQDs has been established. The various functional groups, namely –COOH, C-OH, and C-O-C moieties, present on the surface of CQDs, play a crucial role as favorable nucleation sites for the uniform deposition of ZnO onto the CQDs surface. These functional groups effectively act as anchors for immobilizing Zn2+ ions by forming C-O-Zn bonds. Subsequently, a condensation reaction occurs, wherein the remaining Zn2+ ions are anchored to these nucleation sites, leading to the accumulation of ZnO on the surface of CQDs through ionic bonding.

The schematic representation provided in Fig. S4 illustrates the process, highlighting how oxygen-containing groups on the CQDs surface serve as nucleation sites for ZnO. During this process, Zn2+ ions, initially present as Zn(OH)2, undergo a ring-opening mechanism with epoxy groups and a condensation mechanism with carboxylic and hydroxylic groups on the CQDs' surface. These interactions ultimately contribute to the formation and bonding of ZnO. This detailed analysis enhances our understanding of the intricate molecular interactions governing the ZnO-CQDs interface [52].

In this investigation, the surface of CQDs was modified by incorporating ZnO NPs. Efficient breakdown of organic pollutants requires their initial adsorption onto the photocatalyst's surface. Achieving adsorption-desorption equilibrium involved agitating the dye solution for 30 min in a dark environment, facilitated by the presence of the photocatalyst. Subsequently, the time-dependent UV–visible absorption spectra of aqueous solutions containing MB and CR at concentrations of 5 mg/L were scrutinized under visible light exposure in the presence of the nanocomposite.

Fig. 7A & D illustrates the change in the optical spectra of MB and CR at their maximum absorbance under visible light in the presence of the nanocomposite. The gradual decrease in absorption maxima with increasing illumination time was attributed to the eventual degradation of the dyes chromophoric groups [8,53,54]. As depicted in Fig. 7B & E, it becomes apparent that MB and CR demonstrates minimal degradation in the absence of a catalyst, signifying the lack of self-degradation potential for MB and CR dye. Utilizing the nanocomposite as the photocatalyst, only 65.16 % & 63.12 % of dyes were eliminated even under dark conditions. These control experiments affirm the critical roles of both the catalyst and illumination in the effective removal of dyes. After 30 min of visible light irradiation, the photocatalytic degradation of MB increases to 95.16 %, whereas the degradation percentage of CR increases to 93.21 % after 60 min in the presence of the nanocomposite. To compare the photocatalytic behavior of synthesized materials, the pseudo-first-order kinetic analysis of CR and MB dye degradation was computed using the Langmuir Hinshelwood rate equation:
(6)

C o
C t
= kt
where, k is the pseudo-first-order rate constant, and C0 and Ct are the initial and final concentrations at a given irradiation time.

Following pseudo-first-order kinetics, the kinetic curve was obtained using Eq. (5) [55]. Fig. 7C & F demonstrates that the rate constant value for the nanocomposites notably surpasses that of the ZnO NPs, showing a threefold increase compared to the kapp value of the ZnO NPs. Consequently, the incorporation of CQDs into ZnO NPs can lead to an augmentation of their photocatalytic efficiency. Table S1 provides information on the line formula and parameters related to the modeling of the different nanocomposites (k constant and R2). Table S-2 summarizes the rate constant values of MB and CR with CQDs, ZnO and CQZ nanocomposite, and the first order linear curve fit is represented in Fig. S-5A & B. The results reveal that CQDs incorporated onto ZnO improve the rate of degradation of MB and CR dyes. A comparison analysis was made with relevant data from other works. Under visible light illumination, the synthesized composite exhibited one of the highest activities against MB and CR compared to the activities reported for other materials (Table 1 ).

The analysis of the photocatalytic performance and kinetic plots of CQZ and physical mixture of CQDs and ZnO (Fig. S-16A–D) clearly indicates that the CQZ nanocomposite outperforms the simple physical mixture of CQDs and ZnO. The faster degradation rates and higher kinetic constants observed for CQZ suggest that the enhanced interactions between CQDs and ZnO within the composite significantly improve its photocatalytic efficiency. In contrast, the physical mixture exhibits inferior performance, likely due to the lack of synergistic interactions. These findings strongly support the conclusion that the CQZ nanocomposite is a novel material with superior catalytic properties, resulting from the effective integration of CQDs and ZnO.

The observed differences in rate constants between the CQZ nanocomposite and the physical mixture of CQDs and ZnO indicate that the CQZ composite demonstrates notably distinct kinetic behavior. The higher K1 values for both MB and CR dye in CQZ suggest that this composite reacts more quickly under pseudo-first-order kinetics than the physical mixture. Additionally, the generally higher R2 values for CQZ imply a superior fit of the kinetic model to the experimental data. These findings point to a significant enhancement in photocatalytic activity for the CQZ composite, likely due to chemical interactions between CQDs and ZnO that go beyond mere physical mixing, resulting in improved reactivity and performance (Table S-5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of initial dye concentration on the photocatalytic process
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S-6A & B depicts the photocatalytic degradation of MB and CR dyes at varying concentrations (5-100 mg/L) using the synthesized nanocomposite. It is evident that the degradation of dye decreases with increasing concentration. The presence of reactive oxide species during the photodegradation process contributes to the enhanced degradation activity of the nanocomposite. While holding other parameters constant, the impact of dye concentration on the degradation process was studied at different time intervals. Notably, the MB solution at concentrations of 5, 25, 50, 75, and 100 ppm required 30 min for complete decolorization under photocatalyst-assisted visible light radiation, yielding degradation efficiencies of 95.16 %, 90.16 %, 85.1 %, 76.21%and 65 % respectively. Similarly, CR dye took 60 min to decolorize, exhibiting degradation efficiencies of 93.21 %, 89.75 %, 74.1 %, 66.34 and 50 %. The data highlights an inverse correlation between dye concentration and photocatalytic degradation efficiency. Additionally, higher dye concentrations intercept photons prior to catalyst surface interaction, leading to lower hydroxyl radical production and delaying the degradation process [41,60]. The rate of decolorization is determined by the generation of hydroxyl radicals, a vital component in the decolorization process.

The pH of the solution plays a crucial role in the degradation of dyes, influencing the ionization state of dye molecules, the nature of reactive species engaged, and the kinetics of degradation reactions. As depicted in Fig. S-6C, the modulation of normalized MB dye concentration under visible light irradiation reveals distinct trends at various pH levels. In acidic and neutral environments (pH 3, 5, and 7), the degradation of MB dye appear relatively slow compared to alkaline conditions (pH 9 and 12). Particularly notable is the substantial enhancement of dye degradation at pH 12. This phenomenon is attributed to the increased negativity of the material surface under alkaline conditions, which promotes electrostatic interactions with MB molecules that inherently carry a positive charge. This increased electrostatic affinity facilitates a more rapid adsorption process, thereby accelerating the subsequent degradation of adsorbed dye molecules on the material's surface. Furthermore, in alkaline settings, the presence of hydroxide ions (OH−) is significant in degradation reactions. Acting as nucleophiles, hydroxide ions engage in attacks on dye molecules, leading to their decomposition. Such a mechanism is less pronounced in acidic or neutral conditions [61]. The pH-dependent variations in the degradation of the anionic CR dye were also investigated, as illustrated in Fig. S-6D. The degradation rate is notably higher in acidic media (pH 3 and 5) compared to neutral or alkaline environments (pH 7, 9, and 12). Understanding the distinctive impact of pH on the surface charge of the CQZ nanocomposite material is crucial for comprehending the interaction dynamics in different pH environments. In acidic conditions, the surface charge is expected to be positively charged due to the protonation of surface functional groups. This pH-induced positive charge is believed to enhance electrostatic interactions with negatively charged CR dye molecules. Consequently, this increased electrostatic affinity is hypothesized to play a pivotal role in accelerating the degradation process of CR dye, resulting in a faster degradation rate in acidic media [62].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of temperature and dosage of nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of temperature on dye degradation is illustrated in Fig. S-7A & B. A clear trend emerges as the temperature increases from 30 to 90 °C, indicating a corresponding escalation in dye degradation, suggesting a more favorable degradation process at higher temperatures. In the photocatalytic degradation process involving the CQZ nanocomposite, the rise in temperature enhances dye degradation through various mechanisms. Elevated temperature increases kinetic energy, facilitating more efficient adsorption of dye molecules onto the composite material. It also thermally activates reactive species, enhancing their reactivity and efficiency in degradation reactions. Changes in surface chemistry, including alterations in defects and functional groups, contribute to improved adsorption capacity and catalytic activity. The acceleration of photocatalytic reactions, optimization of reaction conditions, and enhanced thermal stability of the nanocomposite collectively contribute to the observed increase in dye degradation [63]. It is emphasized that a detailed understanding of these temperature-dependent mechanisms requires experimental studies.

Additionally, degradation studies were conducted using varying amounts (0.25 g, 0.5 g, 1 g, 1.5 g, and 2 g) of the nanocomposite to optimize the loading of the photocatalyst onto the dye solution, as depicted in Fig. S-7C & D. The results indicate that maximum degradation is achieved at a nanocomposite concentration of 1 g. Increasing the quantity of photocatalyst from 0.25 g to 1 g led to a notable augmentation in degradation efficiency for both MB and CR dyes when subjected to visible light irradiation. Further increase in the initial catalytic dosage resulted in a marginal increase in degradation without significant impact. The observed trend can be explained by the initial increase in the absorption of photons by the nanocomposite at higher quantities, amplifying the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and substantially bolstering dye degradation. However, as the amount of material is further increased, a diminishing impact on the degradation process is noted. This is attributed to excessive catalyst content causing increased turbidity within the suspension, reducing light penetration, and subsequently diminishing the effectiveness of the degradation process [41,64]. Consequently, 1 g of the nanocomposite emerged as the optimal quantity, resulting in the highest photocatalytic efficiency for dye degradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different catalyst and source on degradation of dye molecules
ce:section.ce:para: As depicted in Fig. S-8A & B, the superior performance of the CQZ nanocomposite with 3 wt% ZnO compared to those with 1 wt% ZnO in the MB and CR degradation processes can be attributed to several decisive factors. The 3 wt% ZnO composition likely achieves an optimal balance, providing sufficient active sites for catalysis and promoting enhanced generation of ROS crucial for dye degradation. The higher ZnO content contributes to an increased surface area, improving adsorption capacity and facilitating better contact between dye molecules and catalytic sites. Additionally, the nanocomposite with 3 wt% ZnO may exhibit enhanced electron-hole separation, reduced charge carrier recombination, and improved overall stability under photocatalytic conditions [10,30]. The synergistic effects of CQDs and ZnO in the nanocomposite contribute to its superior photocatalytic performance compared to those with lower ZnO content. Comprehensive experimental studies are imperative for elucidating the specific mechanisms driving this enhanced catalytic activity. Moreover, it is crucial to note that a further increase in ZnO concentration may impede the process, influenced by factors such as charge carrier dynamics and exciton quenching. Therefore, determining the optimal ZnO concentration is pivotal to achieving the highest degradation efficiency for a specific dye molecule. As illustrated in Fig. S-8C & D, the degradation rate of the nanocomposite was evaluated under various illumination conditions, including exposure to UV, visible, and day light. When subjected to visible light, the nanocomposite containing 3 wt% ZnO NPs achieved complete dye degradation within 30 and 60 min for MB and CR, respectively. Additionally, under UV and day light exposure, degradation reached approximately 50.25 % and 61 %, respectively, for MB dye, and 48.12 % and 55.23 %, respectively, for CR dye. The nanocomposite exhibits a higher rate of degradation under visible light due to the efficient absorption of visible light by both CQDs and ZnO, leveraging their band gap energies. This results in the generation of electron-hole pairs and subsequent initiation of photocatalytic reactions, leading to the formation of ROS, which play a pivotal role in breaking down organic pollutants within the nanocomposite or its immediate environment [49].

Conversely, the degradation of the CQZ nanocomposite is less pronounced under UV and daylight conditions, as depicted in Fig. S-8C & D. This behavior can be explained by ZnOs tendency to absorb UV light, which corresponds to its significant band gap energy. Although ZnO generates electron-hole pairs and ROS under UV light, the overall photocatalytic activity is lower compared to exposure to visible light. Daylight includes both UV and visible light, and the degradation behavior depends on the relative strength of these components. When visible light predominates during the day, degradation is relatively high and resembles the effects of exposure to visible light alone. However, if the UV component is more substantial, degradation occurs to a lesser extent, similar to the situation under pure UV radiation exposure [30,35].

The evaluation of reusability is critical for assessing the practical efficacy of photocatalysts. Fig. 8 depicts the repeated use of the nanocomposite for degrading MB dye molecules under irradiation. The photostability analysis involved four successive cycles, each lasting 60 min, under comparable conditions. After each cycle, the catalyst was collected, filtered, cleaned, dried, and reused for the next photoreaction. Experimental results shown in Fig. 8A, illustrate the performance of the nanocomposite over four cycles of visible light exposure. The efficiency of the nanocomposite catalyst shows a noticeable reduction, decreasing from 95 % in the initial cycle to 80 % by the fourth cycle. This decline in catalytic efficiency is likely due to the gradual loss in the mass of the photocatalyst across each cycle, with an expected mass reduction per cycle ranging from 4 to 6 % by weight. Fig. 8B, C, and D presents XRD and SEM images of the photocatalyst before and after the four recycling tests. The XRD patterns indicate no structural changes during the photocatalytic process, confirming the stable nature of the nanocomposite. The morphology of the CQZ photocatalyst remained consistent after the fourth successive experiment, showing only slight variations in particle shape.

CQZ proves to be a more stable photocatalyst compared to the physical mixture of CQDs and ZnO. The CQZ composite maintains higher degradation efficiency over multiple cycles, showcasing its robustness and long-term effectiveness in photocatalytic applications. In contrast, the physical mixture exhibits a marked decline in performance after only a few cycles, indicating lower stability and effectiveness for repeated use. These results underscore the superior stability and sustained photocatalytic performance of CQZ, making it a better option for long-term applications (Fig. S-17).

The mineralization rate was determined using total organic carbon (TOC) analysis with a TOC analyzer (TOC-VCSH; Shimadzu, Japan). Fig. S9A displays the results of MB and CR degradation rates measured by the TOC analyzer. Upon exposure to visible light, CQZ achieved a 77.33 % and 64.56 % removal rate of TOC for MB and CR dyes, respectively, indicating that the nanocomposite is significantly more effective for dye mineralization. Furthermore, various substrates including methylene blue, congo red, norfloxacin, hydroquinone, and tetracycline were individually tested to investigate the photocatalytic capabilities of CQZ in their photodegradation, as shown in Fig. S-9B. The photodegradation efficiencies are ranked as follows: 95.16 % (MB) > 93.21 % (CR) > 88.54 % (tetracycline) > 42.33 % (hydroquinone) > 30.12 % (norfloxacin). The significant specific surface area and abundant active sites confer excellent adsorption and decomposition capabilities to CQZ for various organic pollutants at these active sites. Therefore, utilizing CQZ as a photocatalyst effectively promotes the degradation of diverse pollutants.

In investigation adsorption phenomena, this study employed two fundamental isotherm models to elucidate the adsorption behavior of dye molecules on a nanocomposite surface. Specifically, the behaviors of dyes, namely MB and CR, were analyzed using the Freundlich and Langmuir adsorption isotherms to understand the underlying mechanisms governing the adsorption process. Experimental procedures involved agitating an aqueous dye suspension for 24 h within a sealed container in the absence of light, utilizing an appropriate quantity of the catalyst. The Freundlich isotherm model characterizes multilayer adsorption on surfaces with heterogeneity, while the Langmuir isotherm model describes monolayer adsorption on surfaces with homogeneity [54]. Mathematically, the Langmuir and the Freundlich isotherm equations are utilized to represent these adsorption models [65]:

where qe (mg/g) and Ce (mg/L) quantifies the amount of dye, and dye concentration at equilibrium; KL (L/mg) and KF [(mg/g/(mg/L)n] are the Langmuir, and Freundlich constant related to adsorbent-adsorbate affinity and adsorption strength; Qmax (mg/g) is the maximum monolayer sorption capacity; 1/n signifies the linearity index. The kind of adsorption is primarily described by ‘n’; the process is advantageous when n > 1, unfavorable when n = 1, and linear when n < 1; the essential feature of the Langmuir isotherm model is known as the separation factor (RL), for which the following equation is used;

Ci is the initial concentration of dye, and adsorption is unfavorable when RL > 1, linear when RL < 1, favorable when RL = 1, and irreversible when RL = 0. Table 2 lists all of the parameters of isotherm models.

The results obtained indicate that the Freundlich isotherm provides a superior fit for the adsorption of MB and CR onto the nanocomposite material, as shown in Fig. S-10. This observation is supported by the significantly higher coefficient of determination (R2) obtained for the Freundlich model when compared to the Langmuir isotherm model, as detailed in Table 2. The values of 1/n, ranging from 0 to 1, serve as indicators of surface homogeneity and adsorption intensity. As n approaches 0, the surface becomes more homogeneous [66]. In the context of this study, the calculated values of 1/n are found to be 1.2 and 1.70, signifying surface heterogeneity and an exponential distribution of active nanocomposite sites [54,67].

To validate the conformity and precision of adsorption data alignment with selected isotherms and their respective predictive models, an analysis of error functions was conducted for the adsorption of CR and MO. Table S3 presents three distinct error functions derived from this investigation: X
2(Chi-squared test), SAE (Sum of Absolute Error), and MPSD (Marquardt's Percent Standard Deviation). The inference drawn from this analysis suggests that the Freundlich isotherm provides a better fit for the MB and CR dyes on the nanocomposite, as evidenced by the comparatively lower error function values for the Freundlich model compared to the Langmuir model.

The experimental results for the adsorption of dye molecules on the surface of the nanocomposite at room temperature were analyzed using pseudo first-order and pseudo second-order kinetic models. The linear forms of these kinetic models are expressed in the following equations.

where, k1 and k2 are the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order rate constants, respectively, where Qe is the amount of adsorbate adsorbed in equilibrium and Qt is the amount of adsorbate adsorbed in mg/L at different time intervals.

Table S-4 summarizes the rate constant values of MB and CR with ZnO and CQZ nanocomposite. The first and second order linear curve fit are represented in Fig. S-5A, B, C & D. The results clearly indicate that the adsorption of both dyes follow pseudo-first order kinetics as the experimental values (Qe,exp) and calculated values (Qe,cal) are in good agreement. Furthermore, the determination coefficients (R2) for the pseudo-first -order were found to be extremely close to 1. As a result, our CQZ nanocomposite could adsorb pollutants more effectively, following the pseudo-first-order kinetics model.

In the context of photocatalytic dye removal facilitated by a catalyst, the study employed various quenchers, namely disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate (Na2-EDTA), benzoquinone (BQ), and isopropyl alcohol (IPA), to capture the reactive species generated during the process. Na2-EDTA, BQ, and IPA were selected as scavengers for h+, •O2−, and •OH, respectively. Fig. S-11A illustrates the photodegradation of MB over a nanocomposite in the presence of these quenchers, conducted under comparable conditions. The introduction of BQ significantly retards the removal of MB, resulting in a mere 11 % degradation. This observation strongly indicates that the superoxide radical (•O2−) plays a predominant role as the primary active species in the photocatalytic process. Furthermore, the inclusion of Na2-EDTA as a scavenging reagent notably hinders MB degradation, resulting in only 55 % removal. Additionally, the presence of IPA demonstrates a noticeable impact on the photocatalytic activity of MB, causing a significant 60 % breakdown of the dye. Consequently, the primary active species involved in the photocatalytic destruction of MB are identified as •O2−and h+, with •OH also contributing to the process. These findings enhance our understanding of the mechanistic aspects of photocatalytic dye removal and underscore the roles of specific reactive species in the observed degradation patterns.

Furthermore, the TA-PL method was used to study the participation of •OH radicals in our photocatalytic experiments. It was anticipated that •OH radicals produced react with TA (terephthalic acid) to form a fluorescent 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (TA-OH) adduct, which is responsible for the PL signal. The rise in fluorescence intensity over time was observed after irradiating an aqueous solution of TA (3 × 10−4 M) and NaOH (1 × 10−3 M) in the presence of a synthesized photocatalyst CQZ. The increase in fluorescence intensity is directly due to the photocatalytic reaction's constant creation of • OH radicals as shown in Fig. S-11B [32].

A probable hypothesized mechanism for photocatalytic degradation of dye under visible light irradiation over the nanocomposite is presented. The conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) edge potentials can be determined by using Eqs. (11) and (12) to describe the mechanism of the transfer of EHP over the nanocomposite material [68,69].
(12)

(13)

E VB

where, Eg, EVB and ECB represent the band gap energy, and the energy of the valence and conduction bands, respectively. X and Ee represent the free energy of electrons w.r.t H2 scale (ca. 4.5 eV) and absolute electronegativity. Subsequently, the CB and VB edges of the synthesized materials were determined, and these values are graphically represented in the schematic diagram. In light of the observed outcomes, a plausible mechanism for the photodegradation of the nanocomposite under visible light was proposed and illustrated in Fig. 9 .

In the context of photocatalysis, the fundamental electron transfer process involves the migration of an electron from the VB to the CB upon exposure to appropriate light energy, thereby generating a hole in the VB on the ZnO surface. The band edge potentials of the CB and VB in ZnO were experimentally determined to be −0.34 and 2.86 eV, respectively. The interaction between ZnO and CQDs through adsorption results in a robust hybridization phenomenon. This hybridization primarily involves the conduction band of ZnO and the π electrons within the aromatic rings present in the structure of CQDs. This interaction significantly enhances the light absorption capabilities of the composite material. Concurrently, the introduction of new energy states, specifically the C 2p electron states of CQDs, within the lower energy range of the CB of ZnO leads to a reduction in the bandgap width [70]. Consequently, the resultant nanocomposite exhibits a narrower bandgap compared to pristine ZnO, facilitating increased light absorption and thereby improving the efficiency of dye elimination processes. In summary, the incorporation of CQDs into ZnO has the potential to augment both charge separation and light-harvesting properties. This enhancement is attributed to the modified electronic structure resulting from the hybridization between ZnO and CQDs, ultimately contributing to the overall efficiency of the photocatalytic system.

Upon exposure to visible light, electrons generated on the CQDs transition to the CB of ZnO. Simultaneously, electrons residing in the CB of ZnO synergistically combine with the energy level of oxygen vacancies (Ovac), giving rise to •O2 − radicals. These radicals play a crucial role in the decomposition of dye molecules. Moreover, the CQDs, integrated into the composite material, function as an electron reservoir derived from ZnO, thereby enhancing charge separation. Additionally, the CQDs exhibit up-conversion luminescence, enabling them to harness longer wavelength light and emit light below 400 nm [71]. This emitted light further excites ZnO, leading to the generation of additional electrons. This phenomenon is accentuated due to the relatively positive VB of ZnO. Photogenerated holes present in the VB of ZnO, in conjunction with the energy levels of the carbon dopants, react with water to produce •OH radicals. These radicals play a pivotal role in the degradation of dye molecules. Consequently, the nanocomposite manifests an increased generation of photoexcited electrons and holes. This heightened separation of charges effectively mitigates charge recombination, thereby contributing to the enhancement of dye photodegradation.

To investigate fluorescence quenching, we conducted a study on the molecular interactions between the nanocomposite (CQZ) and human serum lysozyme (HSL). The binding interaction of the nanocomposite with HSL was examined using fluorescence spectroscopy. The experiment involved evaluating changes in the intrinsic emission fluorescence of the composite at various protein concentrations, as illustrated in Fig. 10A. The fluorescence intensity of the CQZ nanocomposite consistently decreased with increasing HSL concentration, indicating alterations in the fluorophore's microenvironment due to the interaction with HSL. Fig. 10A displays the fluorescence emission spectra of the CQZ-HSL complex interaction, with an excitation wavelength of 350 nm at 25 °C. Furthermore, the binding interaction was observed to intensify the hydrophobic environment of the protein.

Fluorescence quenching in biomacromolecules can be broadly categorized into static, dynamic, and a potential combination of both; dynamic quenching may coexist with static quenching. In contrast to dynamic quenching resulting from fluorophore-quencher collisions, static quenching is attributed to the formation of a ground state complex between the fluorophore and quencher. It is noteworthy that the Stern-Volmer plot exhibits a characteristic ascending slope in both static and dynamic quenching scenarios. The Stern-Volmer equation was employed to quantify fluorescence intensity and HSL binding capacity:
(14)

where, Fo and F stand for fluorescence intensity of HSL and CQZ-HL complex, respectively. Ksv and Kq represent the quenching constant and the bimolecular quenching constant, respectively. Q denotes the concentration of the quencher, and
τ o
represents the average lifetime of CQZ without quencher at 250C.

The slope of the Stern-Volmer equation was used to determine the Stern-Volmer constant (Ksv) for the CQDs, ZnO and the nanocomposite, yielding values of 0.04 × 105 M−1, 0.103 × 107 M−1, and 0.0109 × 1010 M−1, respectively. These results indicate that the nanocomposite exhibits significantly higher quenching efficiency, suggesting stronger interactions with the protein. Table 3 compares the binding constants of our synthesized nanocomposite with those reported in various studies. The binding constant of our nanocomposite is notably higher than those of other materials, indicating stronger and more efficient protein interactions. This enhanced binding affinity can be attributed to the synergistic effects between CQDs and ZnO, leading to an increased surface area, improved charge transfer capabilities, and multiple active sites for protein interaction. The interaction between the protein and quencher was identified as a combination of entirely static and purely dynamic quenching processes. While static and dynamic quenching typically represent two extremes, the Stern-Volmer plot showed an upward curvature, indicating the concurrent presence of static and dynamic quenching in this specific case. This observation was further supported by the fluorescence intensity versus quenching concentration plot (Fig. 10B), which also displayed an upward curve, confirming the coexistence of both quenching mechanisms. Notably, the Stern-Volmer constant was exceptionally high at 25 °C, suggesting a robust binding affinity of the nanocomposite with the quencher (HSL) at this temperature [72,73].

A simple hydrothermal method was employed, for the synthesis of a CQZ nanocomposite, which demonstrated notable efficacy as a photocatalyst. The composite materials showed significantly enhanced photocatalytic efficacy in degrading MB and CR dye under visible light compared to ZnO. DRS analysis confirmed increased optical responsiveness within the visible spectrum, attributed to a noticeable shift in the absorption band edge towards longer wavelengths. The photocatalytic degradation of dyes, analyzed using the Langmuir-Hinshelwood rate equation, exhibited pseudo-first-order kinetics. The synthesized nanocomposite emerged as a promising catalyst model for efficiently mitigating pollutants in wastewater. Adsorption isotherm investigation substantiated that the curves corresponding to MB and CR dyes demonstrated the best fit for the Freundlich isotherm model. Moreover, discernible structural alterations in the protein were identified following their interaction with the nanocomposite, as evidenced by the fluorescence spectra. The modified Stern-Volmer constant, determined to be 0.0109 × 1010 M−1 underscores the efficacy of CQZ as a photocatalyst, affirming its considerable potential for advancing industrial utilization in pollutant remediation and in vivo nanomedical applications.

Qurtulen: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Anees Ahmad: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration. Hesam Salimi Shahraki: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Maidul Islam: Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Mehraj Ahmad: Validation, Formal analysis. Abdullah Saad AlArifi: Writing – review & editing. Rani Bushra: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Formal analysis, Data curation.
